formality.description: Dutch has a T-V distinction: u (formal) vs je/jij (informal). Modern Dutch software increasingly uses je-form, but enterprise and government software still uses u-form. The choice is less rigid than in German or French.

gender.inclusiveGuidance: Dutch has common and neuter grammatical genders (de/het). For referring to people, the gender-neutral pronoun 'die' or 'hen/hun' is increasingly used. For UI text, prefer gender-neutral role descriptions where possible.

formal-u.label: U (Formal)
formal-u.description: Standard for professional software, enterprise, government, and institutional contexts.
formal-u.prompt: Formal Dutch. Use u-form consistently. Professional register for software interfaces, enterprise, and business communication.

informal-je.label: Je (Informal)
informal-je.description: For consumer apps, social media, startups, and casual contexts. Increasingly common in modern Dutch software.
informal-je.prompt: Informal Dutch. Use je-form. Warm, approachable tone for consumer products. This is the modern default for most Dutch consumer-facing software.
